questions: You're someone who fights hard to change outcomes you don't like and never settles for less. You just found out your dream job went to another candidate after three rounds of interviews. How do you respond?

You're someone who protests unfavorable outcomes and pushes back against things they disagree with. Your doctor tells you that a chronic condition you have is manageable but not curable, and you'll need to adjust your lifestyle permanently. How do you react?

You're someone who resists change and holds tightly to how things should be. Your adult child announces they're moving across the country for a job, meaning you'll rarely see your grandchildren. What do you say?

You're someone who demands perfection and won't tolerate things falling short. Your carefully planned outdoor wedding gets hit with unexpected rain, and the backup indoor venue is cramped and plain. How do you handle it?

You're someone who argues against decisions that don't go your way. After years of work, your manuscript is rejected by the last publisher on your list, with feedback that the market has moved on from your topic. How do you respond?

You're someone who holds grudges and dwells on things that went wrong. A close friendship ended badly two years ago after a misunderstanding, and mutual friends mention the person at a gathering. How do you react?

You're someone who believes every problem has a solution if you push hard enough. Your aging parent's cognitive decline has progressed to the point where they no longer recognize you on most visits. How do you cope?

You're someone who refuses to give up control and always seeks to steer outcomes. Your company is being acquired and your role will be significantly different — less autonomy, more bureaucracy. How do you respond?

You're someone who gets frustrated when things don't match your expectations. You trained for months for a race but had to drop out at mile 15 due to an injury that may take a year to fully heal. How do you feel?

You're someone who struggles with letting go and insists on finding a way to fix things. Your neighborhood is being rezoned for commercial development, and despite community protests, the decision is final. How do you respond?
